question: How do you safely bridge a C callback-based processing API into idiomatic Go using cgo.Handle, and what goes wrong at runtime when runtime.LockOSThread is omitted?
answer: Use cgo.Handle to pass a Go channel through C's void* user_data pointer. A C trampoline function converts the uintptr back to a Handle and calls an //export-ed Go function, which sends to the channel. runtime.LockOSThread is required because C libraries store internal state in thread-local storage, but Go goroutines can migrate across OS threads during C calls. Without locking, the C code writes to one thread's TLS and reads from another, causing data corruption or segfaults. However, LockOSThread serializes goroutine scheduling on that thread, so isolate the locked call in a dedicated goroutine. Strings passed to callbacks are only valid during the callback invocation -- C.GoString must copy immediately before the callback returns.
diagram:
flowchart TD
  A[Go Run() creates channel + cgo.Handle] --> B[Go spawns goroutine with LockOSThread]
  B --> C[Go calls C.run_with_progress with trampoline]
  C --> D[C library iterates items]
  D --> E[C calls trampoline for each item]
  E --> F[Trampoline calls goProgressCallback]
  F --> G[Go extracts Handle, sends Progress to channel]
  G --> D
  D -->|Complete| H[Native C run_with_progress returns]
  H --> I[Go closes channel + deletes Handle]
  I --> J[Go unlocks OS thread, goroutine exits]
  
  D -.->|No LockOSThread| K[Goroutine migrates to different OS thread]
  K -.-> L[C library reads stale thread-local state]
  L -.-> M[Segfault or data corruption]
